coredata.dc:title: Development of carbon dots sensor dipstick from sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste toward all-cellulose-derived tetracycline sensor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Alfi, Alia Abdulaziz
Alamrani, Nasser A.
Azher, Omer A.
Snari, Razan M.
Abumelha, Hana M.
Al-Ahmed, Zehbah A.
El-Metwaly, Nashwa M.
coredata.dc:description: Tetracycline antibiotic has been widely used in veterinary and aquafarming medicines to prevent and treat infectious diseases. However, the overall use of tetracycline results in its accumulation in the organism organs, which then migrates to food and eventually increases the risk of developing various diseases. As one of the most well-known contaminants, sugarcane bagasse (SCB) agricultural waste has been a serious problem worldwide. In order to develop luminous carbon dots doped with nitrogen (NCDs), a simple and environmentally friendly approach was devised to reprocess sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste. A passivating agent, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), was used during the hydrothermal carbonization of NCDs in a single-pot procedure. Carbon dots nanoparticles (5–8 nm) embedded in cellulose paper assay were used to prepare the testing strip. With an emission wavelength of 447 nm upon excitation at 355 nm and, NCDs displayed a quantum yield (QY) of up to 24.81%. As a result of the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of NCDs was clearly quenched by tetracycline, demonstrating their exceptional tetracycline selectivity. The detection limit was set in the concentration range of 0–110 μM. Tetracycline is detected and measured using the current cellulose paper strip, which contains a fluorescent signal that could be turned off to eliminate the signal. The fluorescence quenching feature of cellulose carbon dots in the existence of tetracycline affects the fluorescence activity of the paper strip assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bagasse
Cellulose
Carbon dots
Tetracycline
Fluorescent optical sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of NCDs prepared from SCB
Preparation and characterization of NCDs-immobilized strips

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Impact of ammonium hydroxide/cellulose diacetate (NH4OH/CDA) mass ratio on QY.
TEM images of NCDs before (a–b), and after (c–d) exposure to tetracycline.
XRD spectra of NCDs (a), and CDA (b).
FTIR spectra of NCDs and CDA.
NCDs-immobilized dipstick demonstrating off-white under daytime light (a), and blue fluorescence underneath UV light (b).
SEM micrographs of NCDs1 (a–c), and NCDs8 (d–f).
Effect of NCDs concentration level on mechanical behavior of the coated paper strip.

Emission spectra of NCDs6 after exposure to various concentrations of tetracycline; the quenching efficiency (F/F0) was studied as a function of the tetracycline concentrations in the range between 0.01 (a) and 110 μM (b).

Selectivity of NCDs-immobilized dipstick to tetracycline; the emission spectra were recorded for solutions of different competing compounds including amino acids, antibiotics, and metal ions at 100 μM.
Excitation spectra of NCDs6; 110 (a), 100 (b), 50 (c), 25 (d), and 0.01 (e) μM.
Unchangeable emission intensity of NCDs6 at 447 nm, indicating high photostability.
Elemental contents of NCDs0 and NCDs6 at three points (a1, a2 and a3) on the sample surface.
Colorimetric properties of NCDs-immobilized strips under daytime (DT) and ultraviolet (UV) lights.

head.ce:title: Development of carbon dots sensor dipstick from sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste toward all-cellulose-derived tetracycline sensor

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tetracycline antibiotic has been widely used in veterinary and aquafarming medicines to prevent and treat infectious diseases. However, the overall use of tetracycline results in its accumulation in the organism organs, which then migrates to food and eventually increases the risk of developing various diseases. As one of the most well-known contaminants, sugarcane bagasse (SCB) agricultural waste has been a serious problem worldwide. In order to develop luminous carbon dots doped with nitrogen (NCDs), a simple and environmentally friendly approach was devised to reprocess sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste. A passivating agent, ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH), was used during the hydrothermal carbonization of NCDs in a single-pot procedure. Carbon dots nanoparticles (5–8 nm) embedded in cellulose paper assay were used to prepare the testing strip. With an emission wavelength of 447 nm upon excitation at 355 nm and, NCDs displayed a quantum yield (QY) of up to 24.81%. As a result of the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of NCDs was clearly quenched by tetracycline, demonstrating their exceptional tetracycline selectivity. The detection limit was set in the concentration range of 0–110 μM. Tetracycline is detected and measured using the current cellulose paper strip, which contains a fluorescent signal that could be turned off to eliminate the signal. The fluorescence quenching feature of cellulose carbon dots in the existence of tetracycline affects the fluorescence activity of the paper strip assay.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline is widely used in farms and aquafarms as a veterinary antibiotic to control infections as well as promote animal growth and feed efficiency. Tetracycline is the cheapest, second most produced, and one of the most comprehensively used antibiotic worldwide. Abusive overuse of tetracycline antibiotics led to the development of tetracycline-resistant microorganisms in food and animal products, which threaten human health [1,2]. Studies reported on tetracycline residues found in water, aquatic organisms, and daily foods, such as milk, eggs, poultry, meats, honey, and seafood. As most of veterinary antibiotics are soluble in water, animals excrete 17–90% of ingested antibiotics through urine and feces, which are normally used as soil fertilizers in many countries. It has been shown that soils contaminated with tetracycline antibiotics can be genotoxic to wheat plants inhibiting seed germination [3–5]. Tetracycline contaminate bodies of water, reaching up to 20 μg/L in ground water near farms as previously reported by Liu and coworkers [6]. So, there is a growing concern about the impact of tetracycline on the health of aquatic and terrestrial ecosystems as well as human health. Consumers may develop adverse health risks, such as allergy, teeth discoloration, liver damage, or antibiotic-resistant infections. Government regulations in Europe, Canada, and the USA specify that tetracycline residue concentrations should not exceed 0.1, 0.1, 0.3 mg/kg for milk products and 0.1, 0.2, 2.0 mg/kg for muscle products, respectively [7,8]. Extensive studies and assessment methods have been made to aid in the critical task of monitoring and determining tetracycline concentrations in food products and water. A lot of novel analytical methods for tetracycline detection have been developed by current advanced research, including liquid chromatography, tandem mass spectroscopy, capillary electrophoresis, voltammetry, spectrophotometry, luminescence-based sensing, and, more recently, carbon dots sensing [9–12].

Carbon dot (CD) fluorescent nanomaterials can be obtained in sizes lower than 10 nm and consist mainly of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen [13–15]. They exhibit special properties including tunable photoluminescence emission, versatile optical properties, water solubility, negligible cytotoxicity, ease of synthesis, resistance to photobleaching, as well as thermal, chemical, and photostability [16–19]. These properties make them appropriate for various applications, such as optical sensing, drug delivery, energy conversion, catalysis, and bioimaging. Due to their remarkable low cost, non-blinking photoluminescence, and bio-compatibility, carbon dots-based sensors may gradually replace conventional semiconductor quantum dots made of sulfides, cadmium, selenides, and zinc telluride [20]. Conventional top-down or bottom-up CDs synthesis methods are costly, require sophisticated equipment, have tedious steps, involve toxic solvents, and/or require hazardous organic precursors. Therefore, the development of ecofriendly synthesis procedures is growing rapidly among chemistry research communities [21]. Recently, research has shifted toward green carbonaceous material synthesis. CDs can undergo functionalization form ecofriendly sources, like food and agriculture wastes, which conserves resources, provides cheap raw materials, and supports waste disposal [22]. A lot of studies reported on the preparation, characterization, and the quantum yields of CDs made from solid wastes by different synthesis procedures, including microwave-assisted carbonization, hydrothermal carbonization, pyrolysis carbonization, ultrasonic carbonization, and chemical oxidation and exfoliation [23]. More synthesis methods are continuously developing to yield better luminescence properties of the CDs. Monte-Filho and co-workers used onion and lemon biomass for CD synthesis with a 23.6% quantum yield through a carbonization way via a microwave oven for domestic usage [24]. Their CDs were designed as sensors for riboflavin vitamin in mineral supplements detecting concentrations as low as 0.1 μg/mL. Xie et al. were able to use green hydrothermal carbonization to synthesize CDs with a 14.4% quantum yields from Highland barley biomass, a carbon and nitrogen source [25]. The developed sensor was able to detect Hg2+ with a 0.48 μg lower limit of detection. Zhao et al. improved the hydrothermal carbonization protocol and fabricated CD sensors for copper ion detection [26]. They increased the quantum yield of CDs synthesis to a remarkable 67–99% through hydrothermal carbonization of various biomass of hemicelluloses, chitosan, lignin, and cellulose. CDs were obtained by treating the resulting suspension with a sodium hydroxide solution and oxygen. A large-scale synthesis procedure of non-toxic luminescent carbon dots has been developed using large amounts of food waste. Recently, reports on tetracycline detection by CDs were studied with different methods giving different yields. Feng and his colleagues synthesized green CDs with a 13.45% quantum yield from rose flowers for tetracycline sensing by a quenching mechanism [27]. Qi et al. were able to fabricate nitrogen doped florescent CD sensors for tetracycline detection from rice residue as a carbon and nitrogen (from lysine) source [28]. It is used one-step hydrothermal carbonization that produced CDs with a 23.46% yield. Liu et al. were able to produce ratiometric carbon-based fluorescent sensors from Osmanthus fragrans leaves as a carbon source by two different solvent extraction methods [29]. The resulting CDs have two colors of fluorescent emission under UV lamp depending on tetracycline detection. Tetracycline can quench the red color of CDs but not the blue which is protected by a coating of silica layers. Less attention is paid for approaches of efficient large-scale production and conversion of abundant crude feedstock, such as rice husk, wheat, rice straw, corn cobs, and sugarcane, re-growing wastes, to valuable carbon-based products and devices [30–32].

In this context, detection of toxic tetracycline in aqueous environments has been vital. However, the traditional sending methods of tetracycline have been facing some drawbacks, such as sophisticated and expensive instrumentation, as well as time-consuming and complicated processing. The sensors of fluorescence-based have the ability to avoid these drawbacks and demonstrate the advantage of simple operation. Further, bagasse of sugarcane has been one of the most well-known solid agricultural waste contaminants causing serious problems worldwide. Sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste could be turned into valuable materials by using simple techniques. Carbon dots have been reported to exhibit exceptional emission characteristics in comparison to other fluorescent materials as a result their biocompatibility, non-toxicity and low cost. In this study, bagasse of sugarcane as the agricultural waste was employed to synthesize functionalized CDs for tetracycline sensing. Simple, low cost and green method was described for the production of efficient CDs from sugarcane bagasse agricultural wastes via one-pot hydrothermal approach. This method can be used for large-scale production of CDs. The synthesized functionalized CDs were elucidated using various spectroscopic techniques. The quantum yield was increased by introducing nitrogen to the surface of CDs utilizing NH4OH as a passivating substance rather than employing other costly and poisonous passivating amines. The generated NCDs can function as a selective and a sensitive fluorescent “on-off” detector for tetracycline. The impregnation approach was utilized to develop a thin coat of luminous cellulose carbon dots on cellulose strips. Infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were used to examine the produced CDs nanoparticles. Scanning electron microscope (SEM), energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) and FTIR analyses were utilized to determine the distribution of carbon spots on the paper strip. The spectrum investigations of absorption and fluorescence were carried out. The paper sensor strips were also examined for their mechanical characteristics. The current method would not only provide highlights to take benefit of sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste as a valuable material, but also provide inexpensive and green biomass for the preparation of efficient NCDs. This florescent sensor can be used for easy and quick tetracycline concentration detection making it possible to perform wide-reaching food chain analyses for conducting quality control measures.

SCB agricultural waste was obtained from farms located at Qena city, Upper Egypt. Wahttman (off-white) sheets were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Egypt). The sheets exhibits a 11 μm pore size, thickness of 180 μm, diameter of 240 mm, and weight of 87 g/m2. Tetracycline (TCN), gentamicin (GEN), lysine (Lys), amoxicillin (AMO), aspartic acid (ASP), tyrosine (TYR), clarithromycin (CLA), valine (VAL), tryptophan (TRY), threonine (THR), acetic anhydride, ammonium hydroxide, tetrabutylammonium acetate (TBAA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were purchased from Fluka, Merck and Sigma–Aldrich. De-ionized water was employed in all experiments.

Sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste was used to isolate cellulose according to previously reported procedure [33]. It was firstly rinsed with tap water, dried under sunlight, cut into small pieces, and then exposed to milling into a fine powder. The generated fine powder was subjected to bleaching by stirring at 45 °C for 6 h in a solution of sodium hypochlorite (250 mL; 0.735% w/v) to eliminate the lignin content. The produced residue was rinsed with de-ionized water until reaching a neutral pH value, which was then exposed to reflux with sodium hydroxide (150 mL; 17.5%) for 3 h to get rid of the hemicelluloses content. To reach to a neutral pH, the produced residue was washed, followed by air-drying for 2–3 days to provide fibrous cellulose as a white powder.

According to previous method [34], sugarcane bagasse was employed to prepare cellulose diacetate (DS = 2.62). The fibrous cellulose white powder that separated from bagasse of sugarcane as the agricultural waste was slowly added into solvents combination of TBAA-DMSO (2:8). The admixture was stirred for 30 min at 60 °C to provide a clear cellulose solution (8%; w/w), which was slowly exposed to infusion with acetic anhydride. After stirring for 3 h at 60 °C, the produced solution was precipitated by the addition of methanol. The mixture was filtered off under vacuum, rinsed with methanol, and dried under vacuum to afford cellulose diacetate.

NCDs were prepared by using the hydrothermal method according to previous procedure [35]. After treatment with ammonium hydroxide solution, the prepared CDA (0.2 g) was dispersed in water (20 mL). Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) was employed for heating the mixture for 14 h at 240 °C. When the reaction is completed, the autoclave temperature was decreased to ambient temperature. The reaction system was subjected to centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove dark precipitates (by-products). Using ultrafine filter (0.22 μm), contaminations were removed by dialysis for 22 h with de-ionized water using a dialysis tube (MWCO1000). The produced CDs were preserved in a fridge at 4 °C for further analysis.

Different concentrations of NCDs (zero, 5, 15, 30, 45, 60, 75, 90, and 105 mg) were dispersed in ultrapure water (100 mL) to provide different solutions denoted by NCDs0, NCDs1, NCDs2, NCDs3, NCDs4, NCDs5, NCDs6, NCDs7, and NCDs8, respectively. A buffer solution of disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4; 200 μM) was added to the above prepared solutions. Wahttman strips (3 × 8 cm) were then immersed in the above prepared solutions for 5 min, and finally air-dried for an hour. The produced paper strips immobilized with different concentrations of NCDs were preserved in a desiccator for further analysis. Canon A710IS was utilized to take photographs for the NCDs-loaded strips under UV lamp.

The XRD spectra of both cellulose diacetate and NCDs were examined using Bruker Advance D8 (Bruker; Germany). With the use of JEOL1230 TEM (JEOL Co. Ltd. Japan), the NCDs' shape and size were analyzed. A Quanta FEG250 SEM (Republic of Czech) and EDX were utilized to investigate the morphology and elemental composition of NCDs-immobilized strips. Nexus 670 spectrophotometer (Nicolet, United States) was used to obtain FTIR spectra of both NCDs solutions and NCDs-immobilized strips. The mechanical properties of the NCDs-immobilized strips were measured utilizing Zwick Universal (Germany). All samples were subjected to a minimum of five different measurements. A UV1006M031 spectrophotometer (China) was used to measure the carbon dots' absorption spectra. JASCO FP6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) was employed to measure the photoluminescence spectra of both CDs solutions and CDs-loaded strip. Eq. (1) was applied to estimate the QY of CDs in H2SO4(aq) (0.1 M) when the excitation wavelength was set to 365 nm, and employing quinine sulfate reference with a QY of 55% [34].
(1)
QY =
(
I /
I r
)
∗
(
A r
/ A
)
∗
(
η 2
/
η r
2
)
where η is the refractive indices; r is the standardized sample; I and A are the fluorescence and absorbance intensities, respectively.

UltraScan Pro (HunterLab; USA) was employed to capture both color strength (K/S) and CIE Lab color data before and after UV irradiation [36]. The CIE Lab colorimetric coordinates were determined as a three dimensional parameters, including L∗ represented by values between 100 (most white) and 0 (most black), a∗ is the colorimetric ratios between red(+a∗) and green(-a∗), and b∗ is the colorimetric ratios between yellow(+b∗) and blue(-b∗). To measure the color of the NCDs-immobilized strips, samples were subjected to UV light (5 cm above the sample; 365 nm) for five minutes. The color data were immediately collected as soon as the UV light source was turned off.

NCDs-immobilized strips were used to detect tetracycline under ambient conditions in aqueous (de-ionized water) media. Different concentrations of tetracycline aqueous solution (0.01, 25, 50, 100, and 110 μM) were prepared, and then diluted to 50 mL with de-ionized water. A 5-minute soak in each solution was followed by a 30 min air-drying period for the NCDs-loaded strips. Excitation at 355 nm was used to get the emission spectra. In order to test the sensor's tetracycline selectivity, the NCDs-incorporated strips were impregnated under conditions similar to that of tetracycline in solutions of several competing compounds; AMO, CLA, GEN, VAL, LYS, TYR, THR, ASP, Na(I), K(I), and Ca(II). In order to study reversibility and photostability, the fluorescence intensity of NCD6 were recorded in the existence and absence of tetracycline to designate almost no differences in the emission intensity over many cycles to confirm high reversibility and durability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of NCDs prepared from SCB
Preparation and characterization of NCDs-immobilized strips
ce:section.ce:para: Simple impregnation was used to coat the produced NCDs onto cellulose paper strips. The photos of the paper strips immobilized with NCDs that had been subjected to UV light revealed a unique and bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 5 ). Because of its cheapness, eco-friendly, simplicity of manufacture, and convenience of use, the present NCDs emission material can be employed for real-time identification of tetracycline. The off-white backdrop of pristine strip improved the optical photochromic color switchability of the treated sheets to blue emission [42]. In the absence of tetracycline, the NCDs-immobilized strips showed a blue emission under ultraviolet rays, which can be easily detected by the naked eye. The UV irradiation induced rapid and reversible fluorescence in all NCDs-integrated paper strips. In the presence of tetracycline, the NCDs-immobilized paper strips demonstrated an efficient fluorescence quenching detectable also by the naked-eye.

The mechanical properties of the CDs-coated strips were evaluated. It is demonstrated in Fig. 7 that the strips immobilized with different levels of CDs content have different values of tensile strength, Young's modulus and strain percentage. From NCDs0 to NCDs6, NCDs concentration had a significant effect on tensile strength and Young's modulus, while only modest changes were monitored between NCDs6 and NCDs8. The ratio of strain at break hardly altered when the NCDs concentration rose from NCDs0 to NCDs8.

NCDs-immobilized dipsticks were tested for their potential to change color upon exposure to tetracycline. It can be shown in Table 2 that photo-switchable NCDs-immobilized strips were tested using K/S and CIE Lab color screening. The coated NCDs film was completely transparent as both pristine and coated strips displayed an off-white surface. The K/S increased in daytime light and UV light as the NCDs ratio increased, designating improved color strength. Depending on increasing the amount of NCDs, the K/S was found to slightly increase under daytime light due to the generation of a weak yellow shade. Further, the K/S dramatically increased beneath UV due to the generation of a deeper blue shade with increasing the ratio of NCDs. The treated strips were not significantly different from the blank strip in daytime light when increasing the amount of CDs. In daylight, the off-white strips' CIE Lab values did not change due to the low CDs content in the treated film, indicating the transparency of the photochromic layer applied onto the paper surface. The best colorimetric findings were obtained with a NCDs content of 75 mg. When the NCD content was raised, -a∗ and -b∗ exhibited no change beneath daytime light, whereas L∗ demonstrated a slight decrease with increasing the NCDs ratio to indicate a colorless film. The coated strips irradiated with UV light showed a rise in a∗ and b∗ and a decrease in L∗ under UV rays, indicating that a color change to blue.

Because tetracycline has the ability to reversibly quench the emission of NCDs, a “turn-off” emission tetracycline sensor was devised utilizing a paper strip immobilized with NCDs. High fluorescence stability was observed in the NCDs-immobilized paper strip (NCDs6) throughout a pH range of 5–7. The quenching efficiency (F/F0) was estimated relying on fluorescence of NCDs-immobilized dipstick in the presence and absence of tetracycline, which was represented by F and F0, respectively. Aqueous solutions with various concentrations of tetracycline were used to analyze the emission spectra and ability to quench the NCDs-immobilized dipstick as shown in Fig. 8 . Increases in the tetracycline concentration from 0 to 110 μM led to gradually decreasing the maximum fluorescence intensity of the NCDs-immobilized dipstick. Tetracycline concentrations between 0.01 and 110 μM displayed a good quenching efficiency. According to a rule of 3σ, the detection limit was set at 0.01 μM. The current NCDs-immobilized strip displayed a larger range (0.01–110 μM) and a reduced detection limit than that previously reported. Results showed that the accuracy of the present NCDs-immobilized strip was comparable to or greater than that of prior procedures described in previous literature. Besides tetracycline, other compounds that could be present in the human body, including antibiotics (CLA, GEN, and AMO), amino acids (TYR, LYS, ASP, THR, and VAL) and metal cations (Na+, K+, and Ca++) were examined. The fluorescence spectrum and efficiency of quenching (Fig. 9 ) were determined. As a result, the quenching effectiveness of the NCDs-immobilized dipstick was estimated by observing that its emission maxima did not vary much when immersed in solutions of the aforesaid competing chemicals. There was an obvious fluorescence quenching in the tetracycline solution, as the fluorescence intensity dropped by around 86%. The dipstick immobilized with NCDs, furthermore, was less sensitive to the majority of the other chemicals, indicating a notable preference for tetracycline. Possible NCDs quenching mechanisms have been previously investigated [34]. Fig. 2 depicts the TEM analysis of NCDs before and after exposure to tetracycline. When NCDs were immersed in the tetracycline solution, no larger NCD particles were detected. After immersion in the tetracycline solution, the diameter of NCDs was found to be almost the same as the diameter of NCDs particles prior to immersion in the tetracycline solution. Static adsorption was most likely responsible for the reduction in NCDs emission [38]. Fig. 10 shows the excitation spectrum analysis of NCDs aqueous solution. NCDs excitation and absorbance spectra were found to be very well aligned, whereas the fluorescence spectra of NCDs were only partially aligned. The IFE phenomenon seems to be responsible for the emission quenching [34,38]. It was discovered that the NCDs-immobilized strip had an emission lifetime of 7.58 ns before exposure to tetracycline, and 7.32 ns after the exposure. It was found that there was no FRET between the NCDs-immobilized strip before and after treatment with tetracycline, confirming the absence of FRET.

Fig. 11 shows the testing findings of NCDs6 for fatigue resistance and photostability by exposure to tetracycline and UV rays. NCDs6 was immersed in an aqueous solution of tetracycline and then air-dried. The fluorescence intensity was recorded after each cycle. No differences were observed in the fluorescence intensity over many cycles to indicate high reversibility, photostability and durability.

In summary, carbon dots sensor dipstick were developed from sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste for detection of tetracycline. Fluorescent carbon dots nanoparticles (5–8 nm) doped with nitrogen derived from sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste for high-tech applications were prepared. NH4OH was utilized as a passivation substance in a hydrothermal reaction for the preparation of N-doped CDs from CDA derived from sugarcane bagasse. NCDs exhibit a quantum yield of up to 24.81%. By dispersing nanoscale nitrogen-doped carbon dots evenly onto the paper surface, the fluorescent layer's transparency was accomplished. Examining the NCDs fluorescent films deposited onto paper strip was done using a variety of techniques, including SEM, EDX, fluorescence spectra, and colorimetric screening. The produced films demonstrated an off-white color similar to blank paper sheets under visible daylight, a photochromic blue emission under UV light. When exposed to tetracycline, the photochromic blue emission was observed to quench. There was no noticeable difference in mechanical properties between blank and NCDs-loaded strips. Tetracycline was monitored to selectively quench the fluorescence of NCDs. The efficacy of emission quenching was found to be correlated with tetracycline concentration in the range of 0.01–150 μM, establishing a detection limit of 0.01 μM. The NCDs-immobilized paper strips demonstrated high reversibility, photostability and durability. The IFE effect was accountable for the fluorescence quenching. Using the present methodology, we are able to develop useful nanomaterials from sugarcane bagasse agricultural waste, as well as a simple and efficient sensor assay for tetracycline detection.
Not applicable.
The authors agree to publish the article under the Creative Commons Attribution License.
Not applicable.
All relevant data are within the manuscript and available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University Researchers Supporting Project number (PNURSP2022R22), Princess Nourah bint Abdulrahman University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An overview of the main foodstuff sample preparation technologies for tetracycline residue determination
Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Perez-Rodriguez M, Pellerano RG, Pezza L, Pezza HR. An overview of the main foodstuff sample preparation technologies for tetracycline residue determination. Talanta 2018;182:1-21.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Physiological and potential genetic toxicity of chlortetracycline as an emerging pollutant in wheat (Triticum aestivum L.)
Environ Toxicol Chem

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver nanoparticle enhanced fluorescence of europium (III) for detection of tetracycline in milk
Sens Actuators B Chem

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A receptor-based chemiluminescence enzyme linked immunosorbent assay for determination of tetracyclines in milk
